Composable type specifications

Source: vignettes/type-specs.Rmd
type-specs.Rmd
library(typethis)
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'typethis'
#> The following object is masked from 'package:methods':
#> 
#>     signature

A type spec is a structured, composable type description. The t_*() constructors build them; they work everywhere a plain type name does — is_type(), assert_type(), validate_type(), field(), typed_function(), and to_json_schema().

Plain character builtins ("numeric") and predicate functions still work as type arguments without change. Type specs are an additive layer for when those aren’t expressive enough.

The constructors

Constructor Matches
t_union(...) any of the alternatives
t_nullable(type) type or NULL
t_list_of(type, …) a list whose every element matches type
t_vector_of(type, …) an atomic vector of a builtin type
t_enum(values) values in a fixed allowed set
t_model(class) an instance of a registered typed model
t_predicate(fn, description) predicate with a documented description

Use is_type_spec() to detect a composite spec at runtime.

Examples

Union 

id <- t_union("integer", "character")
is_type(1L,    id)
#> [1] TRUE
is_type("u-1", id)
#> [1] TRUE
is_type(1.5,   id)
#> [1] FALSE

Nullable 

maybe_int <- t_nullable("integer")
is_type(NULL, maybe_int)
#> [1] TRUE
is_type(1L,   maybe_int)
#> [1] TRUE
is_type("hi", maybe_int)
#> [1] FALSE

Lists and vectors 

tags <- t_list_of("character", min_length = 1L)
is_type(list("alpha", "beta"), tags)
#> [1] TRUE
is_type(list(),                tags)
#> [1] FALSE

triple <- t_vector_of("integer", exact_length = 3L)
is_type(1:3, triple)
#> [1] TRUE
is_type(1:5, triple)
#> [1] FALSE

t_list_of() accepts any element type spec, including other composites:

mixed <- t_list_of(t_union("integer", "character"))
is_type(list(1L, "two", 3L), mixed)
#> [1] TRUE

Enum 

role <- t_enum(c("admin", "user", "guest"))
is_type("admin", role)
#> [1] TRUE
is_type("root",  role)
#> [1] FALSE

t_enum() is the type-spec sibling of enum_validator(). Use the type-spec form when you want the enum to appear as a true type in field() and typed_function() and to surface in JSON Schema as a typed enum keyword.

Model references

References resolve at validation time, which makes forward references (and cycles) possible:

define_model("Address", fields = list(zip = field("character")))
addr <- t_model("Address")
is_type(new_Address(zip = "10115"), addr)
#> [1] TRUE

Predicates with a description

t_predicate() is a thin wrapper around a custom predicate that carries a description. The description surfaces in error messages and in JSON Schema output.

positive <- t_predicate(
  function(x) is.numeric(x) && all(x > 0),
  description = "positive number"
)
is_type(5,  positive)
#> [1] TRUE
is_type(-1, positive)
#> [1] FALSE

Using specs in models and functions

Anywhere type = or arg_specs = accepts a builtin name, it also accepts a spec.

define_model("Order", fields = list(
  id     = field(t_union("integer", "character")),
  status = field(t_enum(c("new", "paid", "shipped")), default = "new"),
  tags   = field(t_list_of("character"), default = list())
))

ord <- new_Order(id = "ord-1", tags = list("priority", "rush"))
ord$status
#> [1] "new"
ord$tags
#> [[1]]
#> [1] "priority"
#> 
#> [[2]]
#> [1] "rush"
greet <- typed_function(
  function(name, n = 1L) paste(rep(name, n), collapse = " "),
  arg_specs = list(
    name = "character",
    n    = t_union("integer", "numeric")
  ),
  return_spec = "character"
)
greet("hi", n = 3)
#> [1] "hi hi hi"

Coercion

coerce_type() understands a subset of specs where coercion has a clear meaning:

  • t_nullable(...) — NULL passes through; otherwise the inner spec drives coercion;
  • t_union(...) — each alternative is tried in order; the first that coerces and validates wins;
  • t_enum(...) — values already in the allowed set pass through; otherwise the value is coerced to the enum’s value type and re-checked.

Other kinds (t_list_of(), t_vector_of(), t_model(), t_predicate()) raise an explicit error.

coerce_type("paid", t_enum(c("new", "paid", "shipped")))
#> [1] "paid"
coerce_type(NULL,   t_nullable("integer"))
#> NULL
coerce_type("123",  t_union("integer", "character"))
#> [1] 123
